description: This figure presents tensile fracture morphologies of low-nitrogen (LN, subfigure a) and high-nitrogen (HN, subfigure b) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels. Each subfigure consists of three parts: a 3D reconstructed macroscopic fracture profile with height color scale (maximum 12.19 mm for LN, 8.09 mm for HN), low-magnification SEM image of the full fracture surface (1 mm scale bar), and high-magnification SEM micrograph of microscale fracture features (5 μm scale bar). LN steel exhibits a ~45° shear fracture surface, with smaller, higher-density dimples indicating more severe local stress concentration during deformation, earlier necking and lower fracture energy. HN steel shows significantly larger macroscopic necking, a crater-shaped fracture, and larger, deeper, more uniformly distributed dimples, which demonstrates its better plasticity and toughness. These fracture features confirm that nitrogen addition improves the tensile plasticity and fracture resistance of the studied low-density steel, consistent with its higher measured elongation and ultimate tensile strength.
